caveats: a: OEWS combines veneer, plywood, and multiple engineered wood products.
a: The code combines local custom truss fabrication with capital-intensive laminated-member production, so task mix varies materially.
rho: Assistance in structural design requires professional and manufacturer accountability even when models generate work product.
rho: Large integrated mills and local truss shops have very different implementation capacity.
e: The frozen dataset explicitly has no defensible LMM-band firm count; this share does not replace or imply one.
e: The 2022 code includes both former truss manufacturing and other engineered-member manufacturing, with different ownership structures.
s5: No denominator of eligible LMM firms exists for this industry.
s5: Retirement intent is not an observed transaction rate and the proxy is not industry-specific.

q: No public study measures five-year pass-through of implemented AI benefits in this industry.
d5: NAHB covers only part of the horizon and is not a product shipment forecast.
d5: The code includes trusses, which may track local housing starts differently from LVL, I-joists, and custom glulam.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat standardized and project-specific member sales to builders, component dealers, distributors, lumberyards, developers, fabricators, and commercial construction teams, often combining product pricing with design tables, shop drawings, takeoffs, and delivery coordination.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, prefabricated buildings and wall panels classified elsewhere, solid-sawn structural lumber, stand-alone engineering or installation services, non-operating entities, and firms without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. manufacturers repeatedly supplying wood roof and floor trusses, I-joists, laminated veneer lumber, parallel strand lumber, glulam arches and beams, finger-jointed lumber, and related fabricated or laminated structural members to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover broader NAICS 321200, whose largest roles are assemblers, woodworking and sawing machine operators, material movers, truck operators, supervisors, drivers, carpenters, and machine feeders. Engineered-member evidence adds quality-critical scanning plus design-support and project workflows. I translate that broader mix into wage-weighted remaining AI exposure for 321215. The broader proxy may overstate veneer and panel tasks, while omitting code-driven design, estimating, takeoff, and shop-drawing work that could make 321215 more digitally exposed.
caveats: OEWS combines veneer, plywood, and multiple engineered wood products.
The code combines local custom truss fabrication with capital-intensive laminated-member production, so task mix varies materially.
a.change_evidence: A 321215-specific wage and task study split among truss, I-joist, LVL, PSL, and glulam plants, with current automation and engineering-software use by activity, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist defect and bond inspection, grading, cut and layup optimization, predictive maintenance, design checks, takeoffs, shop drawings, quotations, production scheduling, logistics, quality records, and customer support. Assembly, adhesive application, pressing, machining, material handling, maintenance, and certified quality decisions remain physical or accountable human tasks.

d5.bridge: APA identifies housing starts, permits, remodeling, and nonresidential construction as key engineered-wood demand indicators, while NAHB forecasts single-family starts up 1% in 2026 and 5% in 2027 and real remodeling growth of 3% and 2%. I extend these construction indicators cautiously across five years and allow engineered members to gain or lose share versus solid lumber, steel, concrete, and alternative systems. The proxy may overstate demand if housing growth does not translate into member intensity, or understate it if long-span and mass-timber adoption accelerates.
caveats: NAHB covers only part of the horizon and is not a product shipment forecast.
The code includes trusses, which may track local housing starts differently from LVL, I-joists, and custom glulam.
d5.change_evidence: APA's Structural Panel and Engineered Wood Yearbook and Market Outlook volumes for glulam, I-joists, and LVL, supplemented by SBCA truss shipments and five-year construction forecasts, would materially improve the ratio.
d5.rationale: Residential framing supports I-joist, LVL, and truss demand; nonresidential and architectural projects support glulam and other members. Structural efficiency and long spans provide share-growth potential, but housing affordability, rates, codes, fire concerns, lumber and resin costs, and competing systems create downside.

caveats: The frozen dataset explicitly has no defensible LMM-band firm count; this share does not replace or imply one.
The 2022 code includes both former truss manufacturing and other engineered-member manufacturing, with different ownership structures.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matching 321215 establishments to ultimate owners, normalized EBITDA, external-customer revenue, product type, certification, customer concentration, and going-concern status would directly measure the share.
e.rationale: Most independent member manufacturers repeatedly serve external builders or distributors and fit the operating-business lens. Captive component plants, branches of large integrated producers, firms outside the LMM EBITDA band, distressed housing-cycle operators, customer-concentrated shops, and operations lacking transferable certifications reduce eligibility.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 32121: labor compensation (L02) $6,503.8M / sectoral output (T30) $30,680.4M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $501,965 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 32121); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Digital design platforms could shift some service content away from manufacturers without eliminating physical production.
Material substitution affects quantity in d5 and the operator type supplying remaining demand.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of builders insourcing certified member plants, autonomous code-approved manufacturing without an accountable operator, or rapid replacement by non-wood structural systems would lower this estimate.
o.rationale: Structural members require physical fabrication, adhesive and material control, engineering documentation, audited quality, code compliance, traceability, and product liability accountability. Software may automate design and production tasks but cannot replace the responsible manufacturer of the structural product within five years.

caveats: Custom curved glulam and project trusses can retain more benefit than standardized I-joists or LVL.
No public study measures five-year pass-through of implemented AI benefits in this industry.
q.change_evidence: Project quotes, builder contracts, rebid frequency, design-service pricing, warranty claims, and gross-margin persistence following verified AI or automation projects across truss, glulam, I-joist, and LVL producers would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Custom design support, code recognition, audited quality, reliable delivery, and material optimization can make improvements less transparent and more retainable than commodity lumber savings. Competitive bids, builder procurement, standardized span performance, contract renewals, and large distributors still pass a significant share to customers over five years.

caveats: Assistance in structural design requires professional and manufacturer accountability even when models generate work product.
Large integrated mills and local truss shops have very different implementation capacity.
rho.change_evidence: Plant cohorts from equipment, truss-design, and engineered-wood software vendors showing commissioning time, code-validation outcomes, override rates, defect accuracy, uptime, and realized labor-hour savings would narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Production scanners, optimization systems, design software, and structured product standards create viable integration points within five years. Safety-critical structural performance, code approvals, audited quality programs, legacy machinery, project-specific exceptions, scarce labeled defect data, and change control prevent complete implementation.

s5.bridge: An SBA lumber-business case cites New York cross-industry surveys where 57% of owners wanted retirement within five years and fewer than one in five had a credible succession plan. I discount the figures for geographic and industry mismatch and because retirements can lead to family or employee transfers, closure, or continued ownership. The unadjusted retirement-intent proxy likely overstates qualifying control transfers, especially for integrated engineered-wood plants.
caveats: No denominator of eligible LMM firms exists for this industry.
Retirement intent is not an observed transaction rate and the proxy is not industry-specific.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and sector publication transactions mapped to 321215, plus owner-age and succession-plan surveys from SBCA, APA, and the Engineered Wood Technology Association, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Independent truss and specialty glulam firms may face owner succession, while capital-intensive I-joist and LVL facilities are more likely strategic subsidiaries. Housing cyclicality, certification, customer relationships, and plant specificity can delay transactions or produce asset sales instead of qualifying going-concern transfers.

principal_driver: Quality-critical fabrication plus engineering and project workflows provide multiple implementable AI surfaces while structural demand remains physical.
principal_weakness: The missing defensible LMM firm count prevents reliable sizing of the transferable-firm opportunity.
